content:
Hi! The idea that it "rains literally all the time" in India is a bit of an exaggeration, but it’s rooted in some interesting climatological facts.

India has regions that experience very heavy and frequent rainfall, particularly during the monsoon season, which typically lasts from June to September. The monsoon is driven by the seasonal reversal of wind patterns, bringing moist air from the Indian Ocean onto the subcontinent. This causes intense and sustained rainfall in many parts of India, especially in the Western Ghats, Northeastern states like Meghalaya, and parts of Assam.

One famous example is Mawsynram in Meghalaya, which is often cited as the wettest place on Earth. It receives an average annual rainfall of around 11,871 millimeters (467 inches). Similarly, Cherrapunji, also in Meghalaya, is known for its heavy rains. These places can have rain almost daily during the monsoon, contributing to the perception that it "rains all the time."

So while it doesn’t literally rain nonstop throughout the year, certain areas in India experience extremely heavy and frequent rainfall during parts of the year, making them some of the wettest spots on the planet.

If you want, I can share more details about the monsoon mechanism or the geography that contributes to this!
